evidence_text: The paper claims domain-general automatic evaluators can significantly improve digital agents for web navigation and device control.
source_excerpt: "We show that domain-general automatic evaluators can significantly improve the performance of agents for web navigation and device control."
note:
This is an author claim from the abstract, not an independent verification.
```
caveat:
This is an author claim from the abstract, not an independent verification.
```

evidence_text: Across benchmarks, the evaluators matched oracle metrics or human judgments at 74.4% to 92.9% accuracy.
source_excerpt: "We validate the performance of these models in several popular benchmarks for digital agents, finding between 74.4 and 92.9% agreement with oracle evaluation metrics."
note:
The abstract states oracle agreement; later sections report Android agreement against human judgments.
```
caveat:
The abstract states oracle agreement; later sections report Android agreement against human judgments.
```

evidence_text: On WebArena, evaluator accuracy was 80.6% for GPT-4V, 82.1% for Captioner + GPT-4, and 74.4% for Captioner + Mixtral.
source_excerpt: "The end-to-end approach with GPT-4V achieves 80.6% accuracy, while Captioner + Mixtral, which uses only open-weight models, matches the oracle’s evaluations for 74.4% of tasks, and replacing Mixtral with GPT-4 achieves the highest accuracy at 82.1%."
note:
These accuracies are against WebArena's oracle evaluator.
```
caveat:
These accuracies are against WebArena's oracle evaluator.
```

evidence_text: On Android-in-the-Wild, evaluator accuracy against human judgment was 90.6% for GPT-4V, 89.8% for Captioner + GPT-4, and 92.9% for Captioner + Mixtral.
source_excerpt: "Overall, we find that our automated metrics correlate very strongly with human judgment: the Captioner + Mixtral variant shows the highest agreement with human judgment at 92.9% accuracy; replacing Mixtral with GPT-4 leads to a performance drop to 89.8%; and the end-to-end approach of GPT-4V achieves 90.6% accuracy."
note:
The Android reference here is human judgment rather than a benchmark oracle.
```
caveat:
The Android reference here is human judgment rather than a benchmark oracle.
```

evidence_text: The GPT-4-based WebArena agent used as the baseline achieved a 14.4% end-to-end task success rate under the oracle evaluator.
citation: Pan et al., 2024, Section 4 / Agent Policies
source_excerpt: "This agent achieves an end-to-end task success rate of 14.4% using the oracle evaluator."
note:
This is the pre-refinement baseline for the WebArena experiments.
```
caveat:
This is the pre-refinement baseline for the WebArena experiments.
```

evidence_text: Using the evaluator as Reflexion reward improved the best-performing WebArena agent by up to 29% relative success rate.
source_excerpt: "Without any additional supervision, we improve state-of-the-art performance by 29% on the popular benchmark WebArena" and "GPT-4V based evaluator by 29%."
note:
The source reports this as relative improvement, not an absolute percentage-point gain.
```
caveat:
The source reports this as relative improvement, not an absolute percentage-point gain.
```

evidence_text: In WebArena Reflexion experiments, the low-cost Captioner + Mixtral evaluator improved the agent's relative success rate by 16%.
source_excerpt: "We see the improvement our evaluators provide scales favorable with evaluator capability, with Captioner + Mixtral improves agent’s relative success rate by 16% and GPT-4V based evaluator by 29%."
note:
This result is specific to the Captioner + Mixtral evaluator in Reflexion.
```
caveat:
This result is specific to the Captioner + Mixtral evaluator in Reflexion.
```

evidence_text: The paper reports that false negatives in evaluator judgments hurt Reflexion more than false positives because they trigger unnecessary retries of already successful executions.
source_excerpt: "Our preliminary study suggests that false negative evaluations have a more detrimental impact on agent’s performance compared to false positives. If our evaluator predicts an execution is incorrect, but it was actually successful, this forces the agent to retry a successful execution, which nearly always leads a subsequent failure. In contrast, false positives only lose out on the opportunity to retry."
note:
This is presented as a preliminary study and causal explanation, not a fully quantified ablation.
```
caveat:
This is presented as a preliminary study and causal explanation, not a fully quantified ablation.
```

evidence_text: The Android-in-the-Wild evaluation used 120 randomly sampled test tasks, with 40 each from the General, WebShopping, and GoogleApps subsets.
source_excerpt: "In our experiments, we focus on a subset of 120 tasks randomly sampled from the AitW test set." and "The 120 evaluation tasks are evenly and randomly sampled from the General, WebShopping, and GoogleApps subsets of the Android-in-the-Wild test set (40 each)."
note:
The paper says Install and Single subsets were excluded.
```
caveat:
The paper says Install and Single subsets were excluded.
```

evidence_text: About 36% of annotated human demonstrations in the sampled Android-in-the-Wild set were unsuccessful.
source_excerpt: "However, our analysis reveals that about 36% of the human demonstrations we annotate are actually unsuccessful, with common errors including early stopping, completing the wrong task, and making mistakes with respect to the parameters of the task."
note:
This creates tension with treating human demonstrations as reliable references for evaluation.
```
caveat:
This creates tension with treating human demonstrations as reliable references for evaluation.
```

evidence_text: On sampled Android-in-the-Wild policies, all three evaluators had 100% Kendall correlation with human judges, while action matching had 66.7%.
source_excerpt: "Quantitatively, all three evaluators achieve a Kendall correlation of 100% with the human judges, while the action matching score only obtains 66.7%."
note:
This evidence is about ranking agreement on the sampled evaluation set, not universal superiority across all Android tasks.
```
caveat:
This evidence is about ranking agreement on the sampled evaluation set, not universal superiority across all Android tasks.
```

evidence_text: The iOS experiments used 132 designed tasks, with 80 for training/data collection and 52 held out for testing.
source_excerpt: "For data collection and testing purposes, we design 132 common tasks on iOS, with 80 tasks for training and 52 for testing."
note:
The source frames this as a designed task set rather than a pre-existing benchmark.
```
caveat:
The source frames this as a designed task set rather than a pre-existing benchmark.
```

evidence_text: For iOS filtered behavior cloning, the authors sampled 737 CogAgent trajectories from the 80 training tasks and kept state-action pairs with rewards at or above p.
source_excerpt: "We first sample 737 trajectories from CogAgent, conditioned on the 80 training tasks. We use our evaluator to provide per-step evaluations to these trajectories, then apply filtered BC for fine-tuning using this data." and "We set this threshold at ≥ p; i.e., we retain only state-action pairs that positively influence the success of a trajectory."
note:
The reward threshold is defined using the paper's per-step label scheme from Section 3.
```
caveat:
The reward threshold is defined using the paper's per-step label scheme from Section 3.
```

evidence_text: On the 52-task iOS test set, CogAgent completed 8 tasks at baseline, 11 after self-training, and 14 after filtered behavior cloning, which the paper describes as a 75% relative improvement over baseline.
source_excerpt: "iOS device control is a challenging task, with the baseline agent completing only 8 out of 52 tasks, yielding a 15% success rate. Self-training improves over the baseline by 3 tasks. Filtered BC with our evaluator significantly improved the policy model’s performance from 8 to 14 successes, marking a 75% relative improvement."
note: The relative improvement is computed from successful-task counts on this 52-task test set.
caveat: The relative improvement is computed from successful-task counts on this 52-task test set.
